title: About dl-mnist-cnn
tagline: How a 60,000-image dataset, a small PyTorch CNN, and a 4-layer architecture turn canvas scribbles into digit predictions.

sections.title: The Problem
body: MNIST is the canonical entry point to computer vision — 70,000 hand-written digit images (60k train, 10k test), each a 28×28 grayscale grid of pixel values 0–255. Each image has one correct label: the digit 0–9 it depicts.

The classification task: given a new 28×28 image (in this demo, whatever a visitor scribbles on the canvas), predict the digit. It's small enough to train on CPU in minutes and rich enough that simple non-CNN baselines visibly hit a ceiling — which makes it the perfect first DL demo on the platform.

sections.title: Approach — Why a CNN?
body: A fully-connected network sees a 28×28 image as 784 disconnected numbers — it has to relearn the concept of 'pixels near each other matter together' from scratch. A **Convolutional Neural Network** bakes that prior into the architecture: small filters slide across the image, detecting local patterns (edges, then curves, then digit fragments), and weight-sharing keeps the parameter count modest.

The model used here is the classic two-conv-plus-two-FC LeNet-style design. Small enough to train to ≥98% test accuracy in 3 epochs on this NAS's CPU (~8 min); large enough to convincingly handle messy mouse-drawn digits.

bullets.label: Why conv layers
bullets.text: Local pattern detection with shared weights — ~10× fewer parameters than an equivalent FC network for the same accuracy.

bullets.label: Why max-pooling
bullets.text: Halves the spatial dimensions (28→24→12 after conv2+pool), giving the FC layers a 9216-dim input instead of an unmanageable larger one. Also adds mild translation invariance.

bullets.label: Why dropout
bullets.text: Two dropout layers (0.25 after pool, 0.5 after fc1) regularise the network — they make the CNN robust to the noisy, off-center, idiosyncratic digits visitors will draw with a mouse.

bullets.label: Why Adam, lr=1e-3
bullets.text: Sensible defaults that train reliably without learning-rate scheduling. With this dataset size and model, the loss flattens out around epoch 3.

sections.title: Model Architecture
body: Two convolutional layers extract spatial features, max-pooling halves the resolution, then two fully-connected layers map to 10 digit logits. Softmax over the logits gives per-class probabilities. ~1.2M trainable parameters.

output: 9216"] FC1["Linear(9216 → 128) + ReLU"] D2["Dropout(0.5)"] FC2["Linear(128 → 10)
output: 10 logits"] SM["Softmax
per-digit probabilities"] Input --> C1 --> C2 --> P1 --> D1 --> FL --> FC1 --> D2 --> FC2 --> SM classDef io fill:#0d2818,stroke:#22c55e,color:#86efac classDef layer fill:#13161e,stroke:#4f8ef7,color:#e2e8f0 classDef reg fill:#1a1e2a,stroke:#a855f7,color:#cbd5e1 class Input,SM io class C1,C2,P1,FC1,FC2,FL layer class D1,D2 reg

sections.title: Service Architecture
body: Same per-project 4-layer pattern (ADR-013) used by every project on pandyaHomeLab. The canvas in your browser sends a flat 784-pixel array to the API; that's the only contract — everything below the route is interchangeable.

sections.title: Network & Deployment
body: The DL domain has its own bridge network (ADR-016) — dl-postgres, dl-minio, dl-redis, and dl-mlflow live there alongside the demo container. The MLflow tracker is exposed on its own subdomain (`mlflow-dl.pandyahomelab.com`) per the locked subdomain pattern for per-domain MLflow.
No cross-network reach-back into ml-network — domain autonomy means each domain's tracking, storage, and demos are independent of every other domain's.

items.name: PyTorch 2.1 (CPU)
items.purpose: CNN training and inference — CPU-only wheels, no CUDA on the NAS

items.name: torchvision 0.16
items.purpose: MNIST dataset auto-download + standard image normalization

items.name: Pydantic v2
items.purpose: Schema validation — 784-pixel input length check, range check

items.name: uvicorn
items.purpose: ASGI server, runs inside the container

items.name: Docker
items.purpose: Multi-stage build, ~2.5GB final image (dominated by PyTorch wheels)

items.name: Nginx 1.25
items.purpose: Reverse proxy, TLS termination, route fan-out, host-based routing for mlflow-dl subdomain

items.name: MLflow 3.x
items.purpose: Per-domain tracker on dl-mlflow:5000 — separate from ml-mlflow (V3 domain autonomy)

items.name: Synology DSM
items.purpose: Host OS — Docker engine, persistent volumes

sections.title: Read the Code Walkthrough
body: For a guided tour of how this service is built — file by file, layer by layer — start with the **ML Project Walkthrough** (uses iris-knn as the worked example but the 4-layer architecture is identical). A DL-specific walkthrough covering CNN training and canvas preprocessing is planned once 2b and 2c ship.

bullets.label: Project anatomy
bullets.text: Annotated file tree — what every file is and why it's there.

bullets.label: Request flow
bullets.text: A `/predict` request traced hop-by-hop from browser through all four layers.

bullets.label: Training flow
bullets.text: What happens the first time anyone calls `/predict` — load MNIST → train CNN → evaluate → log to dl-mlflow.

bullets.label: Per-layer patterns
bullets.text: Factory pattern, ABC contracts, lazy init, `__file__`-relative paths, the hyphen-vs-underscore symlink trick.

bullets.label: MLflow integration
bullets.text: How runs are logged to the per-domain tracker; why graceful degradation matters.

sections.title: Performance
sections.problem_type: Multi-class classification · balanced (~6k samples per digit) · 10 classes
body: Trained on the full 60,000-sample MNIST train set with Adam (lr=1e-3) for 3 epochs (~8 min on NAS CPU). Evaluated on the held-out 10,000-sample test set.

Because the dataset is **balanced** (each digit has roughly the same sample count), top-1 accuracy is an honest summary metric. MNIST's published state of the art is >99.7% with much bigger models — for a 1.2M-parameter LeNet-style CNN, 98%+ is the well-known plateau.

metrics_detail.name: Accuracy
metrics_detail.what: Fraction of test images classified correctly — the most intuitive measure of overall correctness.
metrics_detail.why_chosen: MNIST is roughly balanced (each digit ≈ 6,000 train / 1,000 test samples). When classes are equal-ish, accuracy is honest and easy to explain. Headline number for any MNIST benchmark.
metrics_detail.watch_out: Doesn't reveal which digits the model confuses with which (4↔9 and 3↔5 are classic MNIST confusions). The next iteration of this demo will add a per-class confusion matrix when DigitClassifier.evaluate() is extended.

metrics_detail.name: Correct
metrics_detail.what: Raw count of test images the model got right.
metrics_detail.why_chosen: Useful sanity check — visually grounds the accuracy percentage in actual image counts.
metrics_detail.watch_out: Only meaningful when paired with the total (10,000).
